title: Levels and trends in maternal and child mortality

scenes.text: Global [emphasis: maternal mortality] has declined steadily since 2000, but the pace of [emphasis: improvement has stagnated]. In 2023, the global maternal mortality ratio (MMR, number of maternal deaths per 100 000 live births) stood at [emphasis: 197]. While this is a substantial improvement compared to the early 2000s, progress slowed after 2015, with a temporary uptick during the COVID-19 pandemic before returning to pre-pandemic trends.

Globally, the [emphasis: under-five mortality rate] fell by half, from[emphasis: 77] deaths per 1,000 live births in [emphasis: 2000 ]to [emphasis: 37 ]in [emphasis: 2023], reflecting millions of lives saved. Yet disparities remain stark: in high-income countries, just [emphasis: 5] children per 1,000 die before age five, compared to [emphasis: 68] in Sub-Saharan Africa.

[emphasis: Neonatal mortalit]y has also declined substantially, from [emphasis: 37] deaths per 1,000 live births in [emphasis: 1990 ]to [emphasis: 17 ]in [emphasis: 2023]. Rates remain highest in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa, which together account for more than [emphasis: 80%] of global newborn deaths. Although both regions have recorded steady improvements, progress has been uneven and levels remain substantially higher than elsewhere.
